aliases.name: Term
aliases.comment:
This type represents an [RDF
Term](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-triple) (which can be an
[IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri),
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal), or a [blank
node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-blank-node)), a [query
variable](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlQueryVariables), or
a [property path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths). They
are used in different contexts, for example when encoding or decoding RDF
graphs, or when building different parts of a SPARQL query, like WHERE,
CONSTRUCT, or SELECT clauses, as well as expressions.

The `compatible` type variable is used to ensure that certain instances of
a `Term` can only be used in the right context. For example, subjects in an
RDF graph can only be IRI's or blank nodes, or, verbs in a WHERE clause can
only be IRI's, query variables or property paths.

For each term variant and all their combinations, we define two type aliases,
one with a type variable and one without, e.g. `IsIri compatible` and `Iri`.

  - The first one will only be used for **arguments** of functions,
  - the second only for **returned values**.

Doing this makes the following code work, as the `insert` function uses the
general `Is... compatible` types.

    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    alice : Iri
    alice =
        iri "http://example.org/alice"

    knows : Iri
    knows =
        iri "http://example.org/#knows"

    someone : BlankNode
    someone =
        blankNode "someone"

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        Graph.insert alice knows someone Graph.empty

Here is an overview of all possible terms and in which context they are
allowed:

```markdown
| Terms         | Graph | Where | Construct | Select | Expression | Path |
|               | S P O | S V O | S V O     |        |            |      |
|---------------|-------|-------|-----------|--------|------------|------|
| BlankNode     | x   x | x   x | x   x     |        |            |      |
| Iri           | x x x | x o x | x x x     |        |     x      |  o   |
| Literal       |     x |     x |     x     |        |     x      |      |
| Var           |       | x x x | x x x     |   x    |     x      |      |
| Path          |       |   x   |           |        |            |  x   |
```

It follows that a term can be compatible or not within the following
combinations:

  - [`Iri`](#Iri)
  - [`Var`](#Var)
  - [`BlankNode`](#BlankNode) or [`Iri`](#Iri)
  - [`BlankNode`](#BlankNode) or [`Iri`](#Iri) or [`Literal`](#Literal)
  - [`BlankNode`](#BlankNode) or [`Iri`](#Iri) or [`Literal`](#Literal) or [`Var`](#Var)
  - [`BlankNode`](#BlankNode) or [`Iri`](#Iri) or [`Var`](#Var)
  - [`Iri`](#Iri) or [`Literal`](#Literal) or [`Var`](#Var)
  - [`Iri`](#Iri) or [`Var`](#Var)
  - [`Var`](#Var) or [`Path`](#Path) (which includes [`Iri`](#Iri))
  - [`Path`](#Path) (which includes [`Iri`](#Iri))

We consider an IRI to be the same as a predicate path and therefore define
`VarOrPath` instead of `IriOrVarOrPath`.

aliases.name: Triple
aliases.comment:
This type represents an [RDF
triple](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-triple). It consists of

  - a subject, which must be an
    [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri) or a [blank
    node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-blank-node),
  - a predicate, which must be an
    [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri),
  - an object, which can be any RDF term, i.e. an
    [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri), a [blank
    node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-blank-node), or
    a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal).

An RDF triple describes a directed labelled node within an RDF graph, pointing
from the subject to the object and the label being the predicate.
aliases.type: { subject : Rdf.BlankNodeOrIri, predicate : Rdf.Iri, object : Rdf.BlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral }

aliases.name: Var
aliases.comment:
An RDF term which can only be a
[query variable](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlQueryVariables).
This type will only be used as the return type of functions and if the values
need to be stored in the model. Take a look at [Term](Rdf#Term) for a general
explanation of the idea behind this.

aliases.name: VarOrPath
aliases.comment:
An RDF term which can only be a [query
variable](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlQueryVariables) or
a [property path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths). This
type will only be used as the return type of functions and if the values need
to be stored in the model. Take a look at [Term](Rdf#Term) for a general
explanation of the idea behind this.

values.name: a
values.comment:
The IRI `http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type` which is used to
state that a node is an instance of an RDFS or OWL class.

values.name: alternative
values.comment:
Create an alternative [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

values.name: append
values.comment:
Append a `String` to a query variable.

    append "Label" (varQ "instance")
    --> varQ "instanceLabel"


    append "Label" (varD "instance")
    --> varD "instanceLabel"

values.name: appendPath
values.comment:
Append a String to the path component of an [`Iri`](#Iri).

    appendPath "/alice" (iri "http://example.org")
    --> iri "http://example.org/alice"

    appendPath "/datasets" (iri "http://example.org?sort=id")
    --> iri "http://example.org/datasets?sort=id"

    appendPath "/people" (iri "http://example.org#alice")
    --> iri "http://example.org/people#alice"

values.name: blankNode
values.comment:
Create a [blank
node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-blank-node) by providing
a blank node identifier, e.g.

    unnamed : BlankNode
    unnamed =
        blankNode "unnamed"

**Warning**: When you are using this function directly, you have to ensure
yourself, that you don't run into naming conflicts.

values.name: boolean
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
[xsd:boolean](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-2/#boolean).

    boolean True
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#boolean")
    -->   "true"

values.name: datatype
values.comment:
Get the [`Literal`](#Literal)'s datatype as [`Iri`](#Iri).

    datatype (string "Alice")
    --> xsd "string"

    datatype (langString "de" "Apfel")
    --> rdf "langString"

    datatype (integer 42)
    --> xsd "integer"

values.name: date
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
[xsd:date](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-2/#date).

    import Time

    date (Time.millisToPosix 0)
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#date")
    -->   "1970-01-01"

values.name: dateTime
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
[xsd:dateTime](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-2/#dateTime).

    import Time

    dateTime (Time.millisToPosix 0)
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#dateTime")
    -->   "1970-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"

values.name: dcterms
values.comment: The prefix `http://purl.org/dc/terms/`.

values.name: decimal
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
[xsd:decimal](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-2/#decimal). Here we are using
the
[Decimal](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/torreyatcitty/the-best-decimal/latest/Decimal#Decimal)
type from the
[torreyatcitty/the-best-decimal](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/torreyatcitty/the-best-decimal/latest/)
package.

    import Decimal exposing (Decimal)

    pi : Decimal
    pi =
        3.14
            |> Decimal.fromFloat
            |> Maybe.withDefault (Decimal.fromInt 0)

    decimal pi
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#decimal")
    -->   "3.14"

values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype

    double 3.14
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double")
    -->   "3.14"

values.name: dropFragment
values.comment:
Drop the fragment from an [`Iri`](#Iri).

    dropFragment (iri "http://example.org#alice")

values.name: iri
values.comment:
Create an
[IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri) (Internal Resource
Identifier) by providing its string representation, e.g.

    alice : Iri
    alice =
        iri "http://example.org/alice"

values.name: langString
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
`http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#langString`. The first argument
must be language tag as defined by
[BCP47](https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/bcp47), the second argument is the
actual string.

    serialize (langString "en" "RDF is great!")
    --> "\"RDF is great!\"@en"

values.name: lastPredicatePath
values.comment:
Return the [`Iri`](#Iri) from the last segment of the given [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

    lastPredicatePath
        (sequence (iri "http://example.org#knows")
            [ rdfs "label" ]
        )
    --> Just (rdfs "label")

values.name: lexicalForm
values.comment:
Turn [`Literal`](#Literal) into it's [lexical
form](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-Graph-Literal).

    lexicalForm (double 3.14)
    --> "3.14"

    lexicalForm (string "RDF is great!")
    --> "RDF is great!"

    lexicalForm (boolean True)
    --> "true"

values.name: literal
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) by providing its
[datatype IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-datatype-iri) and its
[lexical form](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-lexical-form), e.g.

    pi : Literal a
    pi =
        literal xsdDecimal "3.14"

    xsdDecimal : Iri
    xsdDecimal =
        iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#decimal"

There are a few helper functions for creating literals of the most common
datatypes which are part of [built-in
datatypes](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#xsd-datatypes).

values.name: oneOrMore
values.comment:
Create a one or more [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

values.name: owl
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#`.

values.name: prov
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#`.

values.name: rdf
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#`.

values.name: rdfs
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#`.

values.name: rightOf
values.comment:
Return the [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths) which remains after
removing the first path from the beginning of the second path. This will return
`Nothing` if the second path does not start with the first, or if the resulting
path is empty.

    rightOf
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (sequence (iri "http://example.org#knows")
            [ rdfs "label" ]
        )
    --> Just (asPath (rdfs "label"))

    rightOf
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
    --> Nothing

    rightOf
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (iri "http://example.org#owns")
    --> Nothing
values.type: Rdf.IsPath compatible1 -> Rdf.IsPath compatible2 -> Maybe.Maybe Rdf.Path

values.name: schema
values.comment: The prefix `http://schema.org/`.

values.name: sequence
values.comment:
Create a sequence [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

values.name: serialize
values.comment:
Serialize a [`Term`](#Term) into how it would be represented in the
[Turtle](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/) format or in
[SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/).

values.name: serializeTriple
values.comment:
Serialize a [`Triple`](#Triple) into how it would be represented in the
[Turtle](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/) format or in
[SPARQL](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/).

values.name: serializeWith
values.comment:
Serialize a [`Term`](#Term) within the context of
a [`Prologue`](#Prologue).

values.name: setFragment
values.comment:
Set the fragment of an [`Iri`](#Iri)

    setFragment "alice" (iri "http://example.org")
    --> iri "http://example.org#alice"

    setFragment "bob" (iri "http://example.org#alice")
    --> iri "http://example.org#bob"

values.name: setQueryParam
values.comment:
Set a query parameter in an [`Iri`](#Iri)

    setQueryParam "sort" "id" (iri "http://example.org")
    --> iri "http://example.org?sort=id"

    setQueryParam "sort" "name" (iri "http://example.org?sort=id")
    --> iri "http://example.org?sort=name"

values.name: sh
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/ns/shacl#`.

values.name: startsWith
values.comment:
Check if the second [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths) starts with the
first one.

    startsWith
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (sequence (iri "http://example.org#knows")
            [ rdfs "label" ]
        )
    --> True

    startsWith
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
    --> True

    startsWith
        (iri "http://example.org#knows")
        (iri "http://example.org#owns")
    --> False

values.name: string
values.comment:
Create
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-literal) with datatype
[xsd:string](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-2/#string).

    string "Alice Wonderland"
    --> literal
    -->   (iri "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string")
    -->   "Alice Wonderland"

values.name: toBlankNode
values.comment: Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into a [`BlankNode`](#BlankNode) if possible.

values.name: toBlankNodeOrIri
values.comment:
Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into a [`BlankNodeOrIri`](#BlankNodeOrIri) if
possible.

values.name: toBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral
values.comment:
Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into
a [`BlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral`](#BlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral) if possible.

values.name: toBool
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Bool value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:boolean`.

    toBool (boolean True)
    --> Just True

values.name: toDate
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Posix value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:date`.

values.name: toDateTime
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Posix value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:dateTime`.

values.name: toDecimal
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Decimal value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:decimal`.

    import Decimal

    toDecimal (decimal (Decimal.fromIntWithExponent 314 -2))
    --> Just (Decimal.fromIntWithExponent 314 -2)

    toDecimal (float 3.14)
    --> Nothing

    toDecimal (int 42)
    --> Nothing

values.name: toFloat
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Float value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:double` or `xsd:float`.

    Rdf.toFloat (double 3.14)
    --> Just 3.14

    Rdf.toFloat (float 3.14)
    --> Just 3.14

    Rdf.toFloat (int 42)
    --> Nothing

values.name: toInt
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its Int value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:integer` or `xsd:int`.

    toInt (integer 42)
    --> Just 42

    toInt (int 42)
    --> Just 42

    toInt (double 3.14)
    --> Nothing

values.name: toIri
values.comment: Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into an [`Iri`](#Iri) if possible.

values.name: toLangString
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its language tag and String value if
it is a literal with datatype `xsd:langString`.

    toLangString (langString "de" "Apfel")
    --> Just ( "de", "Apfel" )

    toLangString (int 42)
    --> Nothing

values.name: toLiteral
values.comment:
Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into a [`Literal`](#Literal) if
possible.

values.name: toPropertyPath
values.comment:
Turn a [`Path`](#Path) into a [`PropertyPath`](#PropertyPath) to work with
its individual path segments.

    toPropertyPath (iri "http://example.org")
    --> Predicate (iri "http://example.org")

    toPropertyPath (inverse (iri "http://example.org"))
    --> Inverse (Predicate (iri "http://example.org"))

values.name: toString
values.comment:
Take any [`Term`](#Term) and extract its String value if it is a literal
with datatype `xsd:string`.

    toString (string "Alice")
    --> Just "Alice"

    toString (int 42)
    --> Nothing

values.name: toUrl
values.comment:
Extract the URL from an [`Iri`](#Iri).

    toUrl (iri "http://example.org")
    --> "http://example.org"

values.name: toVar
values.comment:
Turn any [`Term`](#Term) into a [`Var`](#Var) if
possible.

values.name: varD
values.comment:
Create a [query
variable](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlQueryVariables) of the
form `$name`.

values.name: varQ
values.comment:
Create a [query
variable](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#sparqlQueryVariables) of the
form `?name`.

values.name: xsd
values.comment: The prefix `http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#`.

values.name: zeroOrMore
values.comment:
Create a zero or more [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

values.name: zeroOrOne
values.comment:
Create a zero or one [property
path](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#propertypaths).

name: Rdf.Graph

unions.name: Error
unions.comment: You get one of these if parsing fails.

unions.name: Seed
unions.comment:
A `Seed` is used to mint unique blank node identifiers. Functions like
[`parseSafe`](#parseSafe), [`fromTriplesSafe`](#fromTriplesSafe), or
[`generateBlankNode`](#generateBlankNode) take a `Seed` and then return an
updated one. You should always use the returned `Seed` in subsequent calls to
ensure that you don't get blank node identifier collisions.

aliases.name: Graph
aliases.comment:
This type represents an [RDF
Graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-rdf-graph).

values.name: addPrefix
values.comment:
Add one prefix declaration to the [`Graph`](#Graph). An existing
declaration with the same name will be overwritten.

values.name: addPrefixes
values.comment:
Add some prefix declarations to the [`Graph`](#Graph). Existing prefixes
with the same name will be overwritten.

values.name: clearBase
values.comment: Remove the base declaration.

values.name: clearPrefixes
values.comment: Remove all prefix declarations.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
A JSON decoder for decoding a [`Graph`](#Graph) from a JSON value according
to the [RDFJS Specification](https://rdf.js.org/).

values.name: decoderSafe
values.comment:
Like [`decoder`](#decoder), but mint fresh blank node identifiers using the
provided [`Seed`](#Seed).

values.name: empty
values.comment: Create an empty RDF graph.

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a [`Graph`](#Graph) into a JSON value according to the [RDFJS
Specification](https://rdf.js.org/).

values.name: errorToString
values.comment: Turn a [`Error`](#Error) into a more human friendly error message.

values.name: fromTriples
values.comment: Convert a list of RDF triples into an RDF graph.

values.name: fromTriplesSafe
values.comment:
Like [`fromTriples`](#fromTriples), this converts a list of RDF triples
into an RDF graph, while also minting new blank node identifiers using the
provided [`Seed`](#Seed). This can be useful if you plan to merge multiple
graphs with [`union`](#union) and need to ensure that there are no identifiers
used twice.

values.name: generateBlankNode
values.comment:
Create a blank node with a freshy minted identifier using the provided
[`Seed`](#Seed).

values.name: getBase
values.comment: Return the current base declaration.

values.name: getPrefixes
values.comment: Return all defined prefix declarations.

values.name: initialSeed
values.comment:
A fixed [`Seed`](#Seed). This is useful for tests. For actual application
code, take a look at [`seedGenerator`](#seedGenerator).

values.name: insert
values.comment: Insert an RDF triple into the [`Graph`](#Graph).
values.type: Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIri compatible1 -> Rdf.IsIri compatible2 -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral compatible3 -> Rdf.Graph.Graph -> Rdf.Graph.Graph

values.name: insertProperty
values.comment:
Insert a property into the graph. If you provide a property path which does
not just consist of a single IRI (or its inverse), intermediate blank nodes
will be minted using the provided [`Seed`](#Seed).
values.type: Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIri compatible1 -> Rdf.IsPath compatible2 -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral compatible3 -> Rdf.Graph.Graph -> Rdf.Graph.Seed -> ( Rdf.Graph.Graph, Rdf.Graph.Seed )

values.name: isEmpty
values.comment:
Determine if a [`Graph`](#Graph) is empty.

    isEmpty empty
    --> True

values.name: mintBlankNodes
values.comment:
Mint new identifiers for all blank nodes in the graph using the provided
[`Seed`](#Seed). This can be usefull before merging multiple
[`Graph`](#Graph)'s with [`union`](#union).

values.name: parse
values.comment:
Parse a [Turtle](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/) or
[N-Triples](https://www.w3.org/TR/n-triples/) file and put all RDF triples into
a [`Graph`](#Graph). This function will keep blank node identifiers and only
mint new ones if they are not given in the original.

**Important:** For minting the [`initialSeed`](#initialSeed) is being used.
Therefore, you will probably get identificer clashes when parsing multiple
graphs and combining them later. It is usually better to use
[`parseSafe`](#parseSafe) instead.

values.name: parseSafe
values.comment:
Parse a [Turtle](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/) or
[N-Triples](https://www.w3.org/TR/n-triples/) file and put all RDF triples into
a [`Graph`](#Graph). This function will keep blank node identifiers and only
mint new ones if they are not given in the original. For minting it uses the
provided [`Seed`](#Seed). Make sure to reuse the returned [`Seed`](#Seed) in
subsequent calls to avoid blank node identifier collisions.
values.type: Rdf.Graph.Seed -> String.String -> Result.Result Rdf.Graph.Error ( Rdf.Graph.Graph, Rdf.Graph.Seed )

values.name: seedGenerator
values.comment: A `Random.Generator` for generating a random [`Seed`](#Seed).

values.name: serialize
values.comment:
Serialize a [`Graph`](#Graph) into the
[N-Triples](https://www.w3.org/TR/n-triples/) format.

values.name: serializeTurtle
values.comment:
Serialize a [`Graph`](#Graph) into the
[Turtle](https://www.w3.org/TR/turtle/) format. Take a look the [Base and
prefixes section](#base-and-prefixes) to adjust the serialization.

values.name: setBase
values.comment: Set the base declaration to the provided URL.

values.name: singleton
values.comment: Create an RDF graph which only contains one triple.
values.type: Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIri compatible1 -> Rdf.IsIri compatible2 -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral compatible3 -> Rdf.Graph.Graph

values.name: union
values.comment:
Merge two [`Graph`](#Graph)'s into one.

**Important:** This will not mint new blank node identifiers, so you might get
unwanted identifier collissions. Take a look at
[`mintBlankNodes`](#mintBlankNodes) for a safer approach.

name: Rdf.Graph.Decode

unions.name: Decoder
unions.comment: A way to specify what kind of thing you want to decode into.

unions.name: Problem
unions.comment: The problem which made decoding fail.

aliases.name: Error
aliases.comment:
When the decoding fails, we get on of these. Use
[`errorToString`](#errorToString) to turn this into a human-friendly form.

values.name: andMap
values.comment: A helper for combining `Decoder`'s in a pipeline.
values.type: Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder (a -> b) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b

values.name: andThen
values.comment:
Run a `Decoder` and then run another one, where you can use the already
decoded value.
values.type: (a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b

values.name: anyPredicate
values.comment:
Run a decoder on the nodes which can be reached by any predicate from the
current focus node. Make sure to use [`many`](#many) if more then one node can
be reached in this way.

values.name: at
values.comment:
Start decoding at each of the provided (focus) node. If more then one of
these nodes exist in your graph, you have to combine this with
[`many`](#many) to get a result.
values.type: List.List Rdf.BlankNodeOrIriOrLiteral -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a

values.name: blankNode
values.comment:
Decode a [blank
node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-blank-nodes).

values.name: blankNodeOrIri
values.comment:
Decode a [Blank
Node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-blank-nodes) or
[IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-IRIs).

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> <#knows> <bob> .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#knows") blankNodeOrIri)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok (Rdf.asBlankNodeOrIri (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/bob"))

values.name: blankNodeOrIriOrLiteral
values.comment:
Decode a [blank
node](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-blank-nodes), an
[IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-IRIs), or
a [literal](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-literal).

values.name: bool
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:boolean` into a `Bool`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> <#isAdmin> true .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#isAdmin") bool)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok True

values.name: combine
values.comment:
Turn a list of `Decoder`'s into a `Decoder` of a list. This decoder will
fail whenever on of its elements fails.

values.name: custom
values.comment: Run the given decoder and feed its result into the pipeline at this point.
values.type: Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder (a -> b) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b

values.name: date
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:date` into a `Time.Posix`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)
    import Time

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        @prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .
        <alice> <#birthDate> "1970-01-01"^^xsd:date .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#birthDate") date)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok (Time.millisToPosix 0)

values.name: dateTime
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:date` into a `Time.Posix`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)
    import Time

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        @prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .
        <alice> <#birthTime> "1970-01-01T00:00:00"^^xsd:dateTime .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#birthTime") dateTime)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok (Time.millisToPosix 0)

values.name: decimal
values.comment: Decode a literal of type `xsd:decimal` into a `Decimal`.

values.name: decode
values.comment:
Run a [`Decoder`](#Decoder) on an actual `Graph` starting at the provided
`Node`'s.
values.type: Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Internal.Graph.Graph -> Result.Result Rdf.Graph.Decode.Error a

values.name: errorToString
values.comment: Turn a decoding error into a human friendly string.

values.name: fail
values.comment: A decoder which always fails with the given message.

values.name: float
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:double` or `xsd:float` into a `Float`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <question> <#hasAnswer> 314E-2 .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/question")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#hasAnswer") float)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok 3.14

values.name: from
values.comment:
Start decoding from the provided (focus) node. You can also nest this
`Decoder` in other `Decoder`'s if you have to change the focus node.
values.type: Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIri compatible -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a

values.name: fromSubject
values.comment:
Start decoding from **all** subjects. If the graph contains more then one
triple, you have to combine this with [`many`](#many) to get a result.

values.name: hardcoded
values.comment:
Rather than decoding anything, use a fixed value for the next step in the
pipeline. `harcoded` does not look at the RDF graph at all.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> <#name> "Alice Wonderland" .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    type alias Person =
        { name : String
        , admin : Bool
        }

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (succeed Person
                |> required (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#name") string
                |> hardcoded True
            )
        )
        graph
    --> Ok
    -->     { name = "Alice Wonderland"
    -->     , admin = True
    -->     }

values.name: inContext
values.comment:
You can use this to mark that you are in a certain context. A stack of
these contexts will be provided with the `Problem` when decoding fails,
which you then can use to improve the reported error message.

values.name: indexedMany
values.comment:
This is like [`many`](#many) but you also get the index of the currently
decoded value. Note that the order in which nodes are decoded is
non-deterministic.

values.name: int
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:integer` or `xsd:int` into a `Int`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <question> <#hasAnswer> 42 .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/question")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#hasAnswer") int)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok 42

values.name: iri
values.comment:
Decode an [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-IRIs).

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> a <#Person> .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property a iri)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#Person")

values.name: langString
values.comment:
Decode a literal of type `rdf:langString` into a `( String, String )` where
the first element is the language tag and the second the String value.

values.name: lazy
values.comment: Use this if you need to decode recursive data structures.

values.name: list
values.comment:
Decode an [RDF
collections](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf-schema/#ch_collectionvocab) into
a `List`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> <#knows> (
            <bob>
            <cindi>
        ) .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#knows") (list iri))
        )
        graph
    --> Ok [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/bob", Rdf.iri "http://example.org/cindi" ]

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#knows") (many (list iri)))
        )
        graph
    --> Ok [ [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/bob", Rdf.iri "http://example.org/cindi" ] ]

values.name: literal
values.comment: Decode a literal of any datatype.

values.name: many
values.comment:
Decode a list of focus nodes by the given decoder.

Note that the position of `many` within a decode expression matters. Say,
a focus node has multiple classes. The following decoder works as expected:

    property a (many class)

However, the following (type-valid) decoder does **not** work:

    many (property a class)

Note that `property a class` alone fails (since there are many classes), and so
`many (property a class)` fails as a whole!

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> a <#Person> , <#Author> .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property a (many iri))
        )
        graph
    --> Ok
    -->   [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#Author"
    -->   , Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#Person"
    -->   ]

values.name: map
values.comment: Transform the decoded value by applying the provided function.

values.name: map2
values.comment: Like [`map`](#map) but it takes two `Decoder`'s.
values.type: (a -> b -> c) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder c

values.name: noProperty
values.comment:
This decoder succeeds if no nodes can be reached by following the provided
property path.

values.name: number
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:integer`, `xsd:int`, `xsd:double`, or
`xsd:float` into a `Float`.

values.name: oneOf
values.comment:
Try a bunch of different `Decoder`'s. It will succeed with the first one
which succeeded. When all fail, you get the error for all cases.

values.name: optional
values.comment:
Decode an optional property.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)
    import Time

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        @prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .
        <alice> <#name> "Alice Wonderland" .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    type alias Person =
        { name : String
        }

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (succeed Person
                |> optional
                    (Rdf.sequence
                        (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#knows")
                        [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#name" ]
                    )
                    string
                    "Unknown"
            )
        )
        graph
    --> Ok
    -->     { name = "Unknown"
    -->     }
values.type: Rdf.IsPath compatible -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder (a -> b) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b

values.name: predicates
values.comment:
Decode the list of all predicates for which the current focus node is the
subject.

values.name: property
values.comment:
Run a decoder at the node which is obtained by following the provided
property path. Make sure to use [`many`](#many) if more then one node can be
found, otherwise decoding will fail.
values.type: Rdf.IsPath compatible -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a

values.name: required
values.comment:
Decode a required property.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)
    import Time

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        @prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .
        <alice> <#knows> [ <#name> "Bob Builder" ] .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    type alias Person =
        { name : String
        }

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (succeed Person
                |> required
                    (Rdf.sequence
                        (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#knows")
                        [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#name" ]
                    )
                    string
            )
        )
        graph
    --> Ok
    -->     { name = "Bob Builder"
    -->     }
values.type: Rdf.IsPath compatible -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder a -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder (a -> b) -> Rdf.Graph.Decode.Decoder b

values.name: string
values.comment:
Decode a Literal of type `xsd:string` into a `String`.

    import Rdf exposing (a)
    import Rdf.Graph as Graph exposing (Graph)

    graph : Graph
    graph =
        """
        @base <http://example.org/> .
        <alice> <#name> "Alice Wonderland" .
        """
            |> Graph.parse
            |> Result.withDefault Graph.empty

    decode
        (from
            (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice")
            (property (Rdf.iri "http://example.org/#name") string)
        )
        graph
    --> Ok "Alice Wonderland"

values.name: succeed
values.comment: A decoder which always succeeds with the given value.

name: Rdf.Graph.Encode

unions.name: Compatible
unions.comment:
You can ignore this type, it is only used for implementing the different
`Encoder` variants.

unions.name: Encoder
unions.comment:
A value which knows how to encode an Elm value into an RDF graph.

The `compatible` type variable is used to ensure that certain instances of an
`Encoder` can only be used in the right context.

aliases.name: GraphEncoder
aliases.comment: An `Encoder` which can be fed into `encode` to produce an RDF graph.

aliases.name: GraphOrTermEncoder
aliases.comment: This type will only be used as the argument of functions.

aliases.name: IsGraphEncoder
aliases.comment: This type will only be used as the argument of functions.
aliases.type: Rdf.Graph.Encode.Encoder { compatible | graph : Rdf.Graph.Encode.Compatible, graphOrTerm : Rdf.Graph.Encode.Compatible }

aliases.name: IsGraphOrTermEncoder
aliases.comment: An `Encoder` which encodes an RDF graph or an RDf term.

aliases.name: IsPropertyEncoder
aliases.comment: This type will only be used as the argument of functions.

aliases.name: IsTermEncoder
aliases.comment: This type will only be used as the argument of functions.
aliases.type: Rdf.Graph.Encode.Encoder { compatible | term : Rdf.Graph.Encode.Compatible, graphOrTerm : Rdf.Graph.Encode.Compatible }

aliases.name: PropertyEncoder
aliases.comment: An `Encoder` which encodes an RDF property.

aliases.name: TermEncoder
aliases.comment: An `Encoder` which encodes an RDF term.

values.name: blankNode
values.comment:
Encode a new blank node alongside some properties where the node is the
subject. This will mint a new blank node identifier under the hood.

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Run an [`Encoder`](#Encoder). You have to provide a `Seed` which is used
for minting unique blank node identifiers. The function `encode` returns the
RDF graph as well as an updated `Seed` which you should use for further
encoding or parsing of RDF graphs to ensure you don't get blank node identifier
clashes.

values.name: fromBlankNode
values.comment:
This works like [`blankNode`](#blankNode) but returns a `PropertyEncoder`.
You can use this if you are deep in a tree of encoders and have to encode
a different/detached part of the graph.

values.name: fromNode
values.comment:
This works like [`node`](#node) but returns a `PropertyEncoder`. You can
use this if you are deep in a tree of encoders and have to encode
a different/detached part of the graph.

values.name: node
values.comment:
Encode a node, for which we already have a name, alongside some properties
where the node is the subject.

values.name: property
values.comment:
Encode a property by providing an IRI of property path and an object. The
current focus node will be the subject.

values.name: term
values.comment:
Encode a single RDF term.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, a)
    import Rdf.Graph exposing (initialSeed, serializeTurtle)

    blankNode
        [ property a (term (iri "http://example.org#Person")) ]
        |> encode initialSeed
        |> Tuple.first
        |> serializeTurtle
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "_:2273e9c9-fa2d-4c28-ae87-947ad36cbecd"
    --> , "  a <http://example.org#Person> ."
    --> ]

name: Rdf.Sparql

unions.name: Expression
unions.comment:
The basic building block of an expression. Take a look at the functions
below to learn how to create and combine these.

unions.name: GroupGraphPattern
unions.comment:
The basic building block of a `WHERE` clause. Take a look at the functions
below to learn how to create and combine these.

unions.name: Query
unions.comment: The `Query` type represents a SPARQL query.

aliases.name: Results
aliases.comment: An instance of `Results` represents the results of a `SELECT` query.

aliases.name: Triple
aliases.comment:
This type represents triple patterns (which appear in `WHERE` clauses) as
well as triple templates (which appear in `CONSTRUCT` clauses). Note that since
predicates in triple templates cannot contain property paths, triples
containing these will be skipped when converting `Query`'s into SPARQL queries.
aliases.type: { subject : Rdf.BlankNodeOrIriOrVar, predicate : Rdf.VarOrPath, object : Rdf.BlankNodeOrIriOrLiteralOrVar }

values.name: and
values.comment:
Create a graph pattern which matches the first **and** the second pattern.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (and
                (triples [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Person") ])
                (triples [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Maintainer") ])
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person a <Person> ."
    --> , "  ?person a <Maintainer> ."
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: bind
values.comment:
Bind the result of an expression to a query variable.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, string, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (and
                (triples
                    [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Person")
                    , triple (varQ "person") (iri "http://example.org#firstName") (varQ "firstName")
                    , triple (varQ "person") (iri "http://example.org#lastName") (varQ "lastName")
                    ]
                )
                (bind
                    (concat
                        [ simple (varQ "firstName")
                        , simple (string " ")
                        , simple (varQ "lastName")
                        ]
                    )
                    (varQ "name")
                )
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person a <Person> ."
    --> , "  ?person <firstName> ?firstName ."
    --> , "  ?person <lastName> ?lastName ."
    --> , "  BIND("
    --> , "    CONCAT("
    --> , "      ?firstName,"
    --> , "      \" \","
    --> , "      ?lastName"
    --> , "    )"
    --> , "    AS"
    --> , "    ?name"
    --> , "  )"
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: concat
values.comment:
Concatenate a list of `Expressions` which evaluate to string literals. This
translates to [`CONCAT`](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#func-concat).

values.name: construct
values.comment:
Build a [`CONSTRUCT`](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#construct)
query.

values.name: empty
values.comment:
Create the empty group graph pattern `{ }`.

    construct []
        |> where_ empty
        |> toString
    --> "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {}"

values.name: equals
values.comment: Compare two expressions. This translates to `<first> = <second>`.

values.name: filter
values.comment:
Add a filter expression.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, string, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (and
                (triples
                    [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Person")
                    , triple (varQ "person") (rdfs "label") (varQ "label")
                    ]
                )
                (filter
                    (equals
                        (simple (varQ "person"))
                        (simple (string "Alice"))
                    )
                )
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person a <Person> ."
    --> , "  ?person rdfs:label ?label ."
    --> , "  FILTER(?person = \"Alice\")"
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: from
values.comment:
Restrict a query to a specific graph by adding
a [`FROM`](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#specifyingDataset).

values.name: isIri
values.comment:
Check if the `Expression` is an IRI. This translates to
[`isIRI`](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#func-isIRI).

values.name: optional
values.comment:
Make a graph pattern optional.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (and
                (triples
                    [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Person") ])
                (optional
                    (triples
                        [ triple (varQ "person") (rdfs "label") (varQ "label") ])
                )
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person a <Person> ."
    --> , "  OPTIONAL {"
    --> , "    ?person rdfs:label ?label ."
    --> , "  }"
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: or
values.comment:
Create a graph pattern which matches the first **or** the second pattern.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (or
                (triples [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Author") ])
                (triples [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Reader") ])
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  {"
    --> , "    ?person a <Author> ."
    --> , "  }"
    --> , "  UNION"
    --> , "  {"
    --> , "    ?person a <Reader> ."
    --> , "  }"
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: resultsDecoder
values.comment:
A JSON decoder to decode the
[`"results"`](https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql12-results-json/#select-results)
section of a variable binding result.

values.name: simple
values.comment: Turn an RDF term into an `Expression`.

values.name: toString
values.comment: Turn a `Query` into a SPARQL query.

values.name: toStringWith
values.comment:
Turn a `Query` into a SPARQL query including and using the `BASE` and
`PREFIX` declarations.

values.name: triple
values.comment: Create a triple pattern/template by providing a subject, verb, and object.
values.type: Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrVar compatible1 -> Rdf.IsVarOrPath compatible2 -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteralOrVar compatible3 -> Rdf.Sparql.Triple

values.name: triples
values.comment:
Create a basic graph pattern which matches a set of triple patterns.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, varQ, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (triples
                [ triple (varQ "person") a (iri "http://example.org#Person")
                , triple (varQ "person") (rdfs "label") (varQ "label")
                ]
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person a <Person> ."
    --> , "  ?person rdfs:label ?label ."
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: triplesAlong
values.comment:
Expand a triple pattern/template with a property path predicate into a list
of triple patterns with IRI or variable predicates. The first argument is the
prefix used for intermediate query variables.

    import Rdf exposing (iri, inverse, sequence, varQ, asPath, a, rdfs)

    construct []
        |> where_
            (triplesAlong "person"
                (varQ "child")
                (sequence
                    (inverse (iri "http://example.org#hasChild"))
                    [ inverse (iri "http://example.org#hasChild")
                    , asPath a
                    ]
                )
                (iri "http://example.org#Person")
                |> triples
            )
        |> toStringWith
            { base = Just "http://example.org#"
            , prefixes = [ ( "rdfs", "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#" ) ]
            }
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "BASE <http://example.org#>"
    --> , "PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>"
    --> , "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  ?person_0 <hasChild> ?child ."
    --> , "  ?person_1 <hasChild> ?person_0 ."
    --> , "  ?person_1 a <Person> ."
    --> , "}"
    --> ]
values.type: String.String -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrVar compatible1 -> Rdf.IsPath compatible2 -> Rdf.IsBlankNodeOrIriOrLiteralOrVar compatible3 -> List.List Rdf.Sparql.Triple

values.name: values
values.comment:
Provide inline data.

    import Rdf

    construct []
        |> where_
            (values (Rdf.varQ "person")
                [ Rdf.iri "http://example.org/alice" ]
            )
        |> toString
        |> String.lines
    --> [ "CONSTRUCT {} WHERE {"
    --> , "  VALUES ?person {"
    --> , "    <http://example.org/alice>"
    --> , "  }"
    --> , "}"
    --> ]

values.name: where_
values.comment:
Set the `WHERE` clause of a `Query` to the given group graph pattern. If
you don't provide this, the empty group graph pattern will be used.
